fragments.content: Traumatic brain injury (TBI) is brain dysfunction from external mechanical force, affecting 2.5 million Americans annually. Classification by severity: mild (GCS 13-15, concussion), moderate (GCS 9-12), severe (GCS 3-8). Primary injury occurs at impact (contusion, hemorrhage, diffuse axonal injury); secondary injury evolves hours-days later (ischemia, edema, excitotoxicity, inflammation). Mechanisms: closed head injury (acceleration-deceleration, rotational forces causing DAI), penetrating trauma, blast injury. Concussion is functional disturbance without structural damage on CT/MRI—symptoms include headache, dizziness, confusion, amnesia, nausea, photophobia. Post-concussion syndrome: persistent symptoms >4 weeks. Complications: epidural hematoma (lucid interval, lens-shaped on CT), subdural hematoma (venous tearing, crescent-shaped), subarachnoid hemorrhage, cerebral edema, increased ICP, post-traumatic epilepsy, chronic traumatic encephalopathy (CTE) from repetitive injury.

TBI management depends on severity. Mild TBI/concussion: cognitive and physical rest 24-48 hours, then gradual return to activity; avoid second impact before symptom resolution (second impact syndrome—rare but catastrophic). Analgesics for headache (avoid opioids initially), antiemetics. Moderate-severe TBI: ABCs, cervical spine protection, avoid hypotension (SBP <90) and hypoxia (SpO2 <90%) which worsen outcomes. ICP management (target ICP <22 mmHg): head elevation 30°, sedation (propofol), osmotic therapy (mannitol 0.25-1g/kg or hypertonic saline 3-23.4%), CSF drainage via EVD, neuromuscular blockade. Decompressive craniectomy for refractory elevated ICP (RESCUEicp trial showed survival benefit but more vegetative state survivors). Seizure prophylaxis: levetiracetam or phenytoin × 7 days only (prevents early seizures, not late). Rehabilitation: early mobilization, PT/OT/speech therapy, cognitive rehabilitation. Chronic issues: spasticity (baclofen, botulinum toxin), post-traumatic headache, mood disorders, cognitive impairment, post-traumatic hypopituitarism (screen for GH, gonadotropin deficiency).

TBI diagnosis uses Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS, score 3-15): Eye opening (1-4), Verbal response (1-5), Motor response (1-6). GCS 13-15 = mild, 9-12 = moderate, 3-8 = severe (intubate if ≤8). Canadian CT Head Rule/New Orleans Criteria guide imaging in mild TBI. Non-contrast CT head emergently for: GCS <15, focal deficit, vomiting, severe headache, age >65, coagulopathy, dangerous mechanism, loss of consciousness/amnesia. CT findings: skull fracture, intracranial hemorrhage (epidural, subdural, subarachnoid, intraparenchymal), cerebral edema, midline shift, mass effect. MRI more sensitive for diffuse axonal injury (microhemorrhages on SWI/GRE), small contusions. Diffusion tensor imaging (DTI) research tool for white matter integrity. Biomarkers under investigation: GFAP, UCH-L1 (FDA-cleared to rule out need for CT in mild TBI). Marshall/Rotterdam CT scores quantify injury severity and predict outcome. Differential diagnosis: stroke, seizure/postictal state, intoxication, metabolic encephalopathy. Prognosis: GCS motor score most predictive; pupillary reactivity important; IMPACT/CRASH calculators estimate outcome.
